speaker: interviewer
text: Be right back. I have to run to the car for one second.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's got a lot of pictures?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We would want to find out so we'll know when to start.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So we were unloading turnips, interrupted by an air attack. And what I did-- stupid as I am-- I put my hand out and grabbed one turnip. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the SS people, who were the guards who were with us, otherwise hadn't really much taken attention of what we do or don't do. They were just standing around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the alarm was, over, [INAUDIBLE] down, somewhere was fire. One of the SS men came to me and said, you know what it means during an air alarm to steal? And I said, I don't steal. That was just one turnip, [? Victor. ?] That is stealing. And for this, it's the death penalty without any court hearing or delay. [SIGHS] So I went back to unload turnips until the day was over. We all were transported by a truck to our camp. And now I expected the worst.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very friendly-- there was another doctor whom I vaguely knew in Prague, but then we were together. He was a dentist, and we pretty much spent the last weeks of the war together. I told him this story, and he said, nonsense. And I just repaired the teeth of this SS man there, and I will talk with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he talks with him, and he said, forget about it. But that was about six hours later, another of the crucial points. Saved by a rotten tooth. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Very soon after this, the front came very close. The American army was marching close, and we had to evacuate our camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were just put on the street and told, with scouts on both sides, walk. We walked through the inside of the country. The American army moving closer and closer, I mean, shooting our missiles, some fireballs right and left. And hoped that this might be the end. But that walk took three days. No food. At night, they let us sleep, wherever we were. Just get down, and early in the morning, get up again. There was a little bit grass we could take and chew it. But again, the thirst was unbearable. We walked through a little village, a little German village between our camp and [? Yena ?]. It's the same [? Yena-- ?] we talked about it before-- where the [INAUDIBLE] and [? Leica ?] factories were. And people were looking out the windows and looking down at this poor rotten marching person was there. And we only said one thing, Wasser, water, water. We didn't want anything else but water. But they closed the windows. They wouldn't dare or wouldn't want. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been about the third-- after the third night, or in the evening of the third day. That we say something-- saw something funny happening. The SS guards, which we by now knew, suddenly had different clothes. They had thrown away their SS uniforms and were in regular military uniforms. Because the American army did not recognize the SS as prisoners of war when they're caught, see? They were criminals because of their involvement in-- in their torturing, and so on. So now we had the same people in a different uniform. And started to talk friendly with us. There was one man who was marching next to me who said he used to be in-- a director at a playhouse in a small German town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, all these good Jewish authors we could play there, that all was gone now. Now maybe the war comes to an end, and we can go back to a normal life. Now, that wasn't the way people talked to-- Germans, SS people-- talked to us. We knew that they had order just to save their back. And then it took an hour or so under their gun. I don't know where they were ended. They push us into the forest. We were free. We were free, but we didn't know where we were and what we were. Now, I don't want to tell you details. There are many little details. One that hit me very much, there was a Hungarian doctor who-- with us. He was in camp with us and had helped us, and I had helped him. And he marched next to us. And he looked like my father, was at the age of my father, and I was rather protective of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a little prostate trouble, so he-- he had to urinate. So he asked the guard, said, the most extreme, can I step out? And he went, [INAUDIBLE], sure. And he stepped out, and in the meantime, the whole thing moved on. The next guard comes and shot him down.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very tragic. It was a day before liberation, before the end of all this. And I don't know why this is more impressive to me than many other things, but I wanted to tell it. But by now we are back, we are free, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you became free?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a shack in the field. And we were, of course, exhausted. And there were people, obviously prisoners, according to the uniform, going in and out. They  called us, made us signs to come and join them. They were French prisoners there. Each also got free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went in, and they had a little bit-- some waters, there, and then some bread-- and gave us and shared with us what they had. And we were totally exhausted. We fell down in the deepest sleep. We didn't know for-- what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I woke up and saw that it was very early. And some light came in. And I heard cars-- or not cars, a motor, so for motorized units on the street. I didn't know what it was. But I jumped out of that cottage, took Fritz Pearl, my friend who was with me, out, and said, look, they are here. And there was American tanks, helicopters, motorcycle units moving slowly from west into the inner of Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We ran down to the street, and I spoke enough English to be able to communicate. Fritz knew a little bit of English, so we communicated with the officers. They after immediately telephoned backward to the headquarters. There is a group of prisoners, they say. And they got the order, tell them to go back-- to come to headquarters. We will take care of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- there moved Austria eastward-- and we walked very slowly westward until we got to Vienna, the city. So the next city was the headquarter of this unit. And they took care of us. They took care of us by sending-- sending us to an empty little camp on the periphery and gave-- the officers that were in charge of that community, American officers-- gave order to the Germans to give us everything we need, and food, and clothes, and medication. And one little moment, and then I stop, because-- Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we made it. And I really was just laying in the grass and couldn't move. And I didn't think I had any initiative to be happy. I knew I was free. And that Fritz Pearl, my friend, comes and said, listen, you cannot do this. I think we have a case of typhoid here. We have to start taking care of our friends. And I got up, and we created a little hospital in this camp. And where--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reconstitute yourself psychologically?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, critical moment was that there, I'm needed for something, and that helped. I didn't think I have the initiative now to start life, but called to service, in a way, gave me the push to start. That was the start.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: '50-- I must say, once before, but when I immigrated to America was '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between '45 and '56, you got your degrees--

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your training.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, back to Prague. After I got my degree, it was great bureaucratic difficulties under Czech University, because they came from a German. Had a great teaching position at a neurological clinic in Prague. And there came the new creation of the state of Israel. And they needed doctors for the military. And I, by then, married and with with a newborn, first-year child, went to Israel, where I stayed for seven years. Needless to say is that I waited for the visa to America from '38 to '56. I mean, even then I would have wanted to go, because my sister was already here, which is a different story. And I wanted the way to go here. But I had to wait seven more years. But really, seven very productive years in my profession is also-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, what happened to you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I had to fight with new bureaucratic difficulties. It took me five years to get a license. For just bureaucratic difficulties. See, examinations I had to, tests I had to make, there were no problems. Two years of internship in a hospital here. I liked working, even as it was general physician, was no problem. But to overcome the bureaucratic difficulties was nearly impossible. Now, after five years, I got it. I had to prove that if an American doctor would go to-- sent Czechoslovakia, he would be able to get a license to work as a doctor. A hypothetical case, nobody ever did it. And only when I could prove that such proof is impossible to deliver, they waived it. But this is the least.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married. And did-- did you then divorce and marry again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was married-- after the war, very soon, I married to a woman with whom I was in kind of a, ah, what should I say? Engaged, if you want to say, before the war. But of course, there was a long time in-between. We married and immediately had a child. It was not a very happy time for each of us-- of us, because, you know, I think continuation was interrupted. But we stayed married because of the child for 24 years. And then finally, divorced in '71. And she later married again, and I am married again. But my second wife then died after seven years. She was young but had cancer suddenly, a very malignant, fast-growing cancer, and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you married for the third time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I married for the third time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would very much like to introduce-- you to introduce her to us. Would you introduce your wife for us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Can I introduce you? This is my wife Rose, Roslyn. We are married for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Really? 16 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are also in psychology.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm a therapist.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're a therapist. And tell-- tell us, how did his war experiences, how did the Holocaust impact on-- on his life from your point of view in your relationship? Can you say something about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Well, I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] personally, in-- in our personal life, I think there have been some problems in terms of memories and-- and things that have come up at various times. Sometimes in sleeping, and that's perhaps on the more painful side. But on the more positive side, I think that we have both done quite a lot of work with survivors. I worked for several years with displaced persons after the war. And I think that his experiences in the war gave me another view of how people cope with things. He is the least bitter person that I have ever known who came out of the war. And I think it gave me a fuller perspective on how people can connect their lives again, and what they can deal with in a more optimistic way. That there is still a life ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How do you account for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, professionally, I would say that I think he had a very good early life [LAUGHS] with his parents. And I don't know the other answers. Personality, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't ask you about dreaming. But Rosalyn is suggesting that you have sleep disturbances. Are these dreams that you can recall later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Often. No, not often, but at times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Less and less, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- by now, really much less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these dreams over the years, have they-- what did they have to do with, generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have usually to do with expecting some disaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Expecting disaster.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, like one-- like I was in one of these crucial moments when I really thought, it is the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your experiences in the Holocaust propel you towards psychiatry, or was that something that you had decided on way before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did before. I did before the war, but it's somehow given us another facet to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't you think that you work exceptionally well with older people who have also been Holocaust survivors in their aging process?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is another special work I do at the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you both very much, and I want to especially thank you. And I would like very much to have a chance to see some photographs that you have, so that you can tell us and show us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have to go up to bring them down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother from mother's side, Helena Birnbaum, who was a great lady. A pianist. Very educated person, and played a great role in my growing up. She was the ideal of a woman for me. Widowed very early in life and never married again. More?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Was she your-- the one who hugged you and loved you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I mean, my mother too. But she-- she was always-- you know, when I was born, my father was in the military. And there were three women around me, my mother, my grandmother, and the nurse, or whatever she was, [INAUDIBLE]. And this was really my world in growing up. You can imagine I talked a lot about it in analysis. But she is the one who gave me a lot of understanding, knowledge, and appreciation for music and literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is a picture of my parents, probably taken in 1914 or '15, when my father was in the military service as a volunteer and fighting for Austria in Sarajevo. With my mother, who had already delivered me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was he away fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, he was nearly during the whole war, on and off. He got-- he got shot, was injured, came home for some vacation, went back. He had had a great love for the old Austrian Hungarian monarchy. Didn't help very much later on in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herta Eva Birnbaum and Max Bloch.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the marriage certificate of my parents. They were married in June, I think, the 2nd, 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Teplitz. It's the same city where I was born. At that time, it was called Teplitz-Schonau, which are two places that are combined. Today, it is called Teplice in Bohemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is my mother and I. I can only guess how old, probably in the first year. I mean, I have big eyes and two big ears. That's all I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in October '14, so it might be early '15.

speaker: interviewee
text: I may be, you know, half or three-quarter of a year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Probably around March of 1915--

speaker: interviewee
text: I certainly was, as I can see, surprised about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also in the same city--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same city, at the same place where  we had the other picture. The beautiful city of Teplitz-Schonau. The first person is my mother, Helta Bloch, nee Birnbaum. Next to her is-- am I. Then comes my uncle, Sigmund Bloch. My grandmother, the mother of my father, and Sigmund, and others. I think it was my grandmother's 70th birthday. Her name was Josephine Bloch. And at the moment, I can't remember what her-- what [? born ?] she was. But she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's taken in our home in Teplitz, where the whole family from different cities came together to celebrate her 70th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of those people survive, other than yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister survived. By now, she-- which is the one on the other end, on the left end. At that time, must have been, I would say, 15, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what was  her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanna. Hanna Bloch. Then she was married-- Kohner. K-O-H-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Bloch. She [? invited ?] me. She wrote a book about her Hollywood-- ah, Holocaust-- experiences, which was printed and translated into many languages, very successful. In our camp in Birkenau, the day before liquidation and execution of nearly all people, we were receiving these postcards and were supposed to send some to anybody we knew who was free and in Germany or our former homeland, Bohemia, Czechoslovakia. And the next day, most of the people were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what purpose did-- were you supposed to send those?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't explain to us. Just send it to them so they will know where you are, they can send you packages. But what they really did is they wanted to prove that we are alive. It was the last day of our life, of the life of most of the people. What good it did, I don't know. But there was always an attempt to cover up for the terrible killing. And that was very obvious. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The postcards were, of course, censored, and we knew this. And they made it clear that when we write something that they wouldn't like, it would be just destroyed. The upper one says, Dear-- or Meine Liebe, I say-- My Dear. And says, I am OK. I am in a camp where I work as a doctor. And I appreciate very much the bread you send to me, or whatever edible you can send, in the future will be very important for me. I hope you are well. Best greetings, Friedl. That was the way-- I am Fred now. The second one is shorter. That was already before the camp was finally destroyed. And again, it says, this time, Leibe Friedl. Or-- yes. I just want to let you know that I am well. And everything is going as before. I hope to hear from you. I hope you stay well, Friedl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these postcards were sent to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sent to the woman who then was my first wife, became my first wife after the war. And she still was at the same address. He was-- her father was Jewish, her mother was German. So she could stay outside any kind of imprisonment. Had a hard time, but managed to survive. This is a first certificate given by the concentration camp Buchenwald after the liberation, which-- which is an identification card, because it was a very wild time. And all kinds of people were running in Germany around, and you never who was friend and who is enemy. This helped me when I came back to Prague to prove who I am. And then the usual bureaucracy started. Finished. This is a red cross that personnels, physicians in the concentration camp Buchenwald, were carrying. They had some freer movement because of it. Now I got it after the liberation and have a special certificate that explains it. It is written on it, [INAUDIBLE] Buchenwald. [? Right ?] I [INAUDIBLE] were personnels Hospital Buchenwald. Buchenwald was in [INAUDIBLE]. This is a certificate from Dr. Holmes, the American physician. I think he was a major in the army. He said, I am entitled to have this red cross on my arm with all the privileges that belong to it. Thank you. This is my daughter, [? Ilana ?] Helen [? Bloch-Light. ?] Her husband is Ben [? Light. ?] Taken in Los Angeles in our garden about five years ago, I would guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Prague in '46. February '46. [INAUDIBLE]. Went to elementary school in Israel, in [? Gan Shlomo. ?] And then we came to Los Angeles, where she had her high school and college training. She is an artist, a painter. Teaches art and sells her art, and is quite successful, but not rich.
